get-rid-of-waste.md:
## Schedule pickup {#schedule-pickup}

Regular trash and recycling pickup is available for residents. Check your zone and schedule online on the city’s sanitation or public works website—enter your address to find your collection day and any holiday changes. Place bins at the curb by the listed time on your collection day (e.g., 6 a.m. or the night before if allowed). Bins should be accessible and not blocked by vehicles.

Trash is typically collected weekly or twice weekly; recycling may be weekly or every other week depending on your area. Yard waste (leaves, grass, branches) may have a separate schedule or require special bags or containers. Missed pickups can often be reported online or by calling 311; have your address and collection day ready.

## Bulk items {#bulk-items}

Large items (furniture, mattresses, appliances, electronics) often require special pickup and may not be collected with regular trash. Schedule a bulk pickup through the online portal or by calling 311. There may be a fee for certain items (e.g., mattresses, appliances); the website or operator will explain. Items are usually collected on a designated day; place them at the curb (or as instructed) by the required time. Some items (e.g., refrigerators, TVs) must be recycled or disposed of at a designated facility—check the city’s waste guide for rules.

Donation is an alternative for items in good condition; many charities accept furniture, appliances, and clothing and may offer free pickup.

## Recycling {#recycling}

Recycling guidelines vary by material and by location. Common accepted items include:

- Paper and cardboard — Newspaper, magazines, office paper, cardboard boxes (flattened). Often no plastic bags or food-soiled paper.
- Plastic — Bottles and containers (check for the resin number; many programs accept #1 and #2). Lids and labels may or may not be accepted—follow local rules.
- Metal — Aluminum and steel cans. Rinse to avoid contamination.
- Glass — Bottles and jars in some areas; others have dropped glass or use separate collection.

Preparation matters: rinse containers, remove caps if required, and do not bag recyclables in plastic bags unless your program allows it. Contamination (e.g., food, non-recyclables) can cause entire loads to be rejected. Check the local recycling guide on the city website for the full list, preparation instructions, and what to keep out of the bin. When in doubt, leave it out or check the guide.

Hazardous waste (paint, chemicals, batteries, electronics) usually cannot go in curbside recycling or trash; use designated drop-off events or facilities. Dates and locations are typically posted on the city website.
illegal-parking-complaint.md:
## Report illegal parking

Report vehicles that are parked in no-parking zones, blocking driveways, or violating other parking rules. You can submit a report online with the location and, if possible, the license plate and a photo.

## What to include

- Exact address or cross streets
- License plate number (if visible)
- Description of the violation
- Date and time
- Photo if available

Enforcement response depends on availability and priority. For vehicles blocking access or creating a safety hazard, you may also contact non-emergency dispatch.
marriage-licenses.md:
## Apply for a license

Apply for a marriage license in person at the clerk’s office or vital records office in the jurisdiction where you plan to marry. Some localities allow you to start the process online but require in-person completion. Both partners usually must appear and present valid ID.

## Requirements

Requirements vary by location but often include proof of identity and age, and dissolution or death certificates if either party was previously married. Some places require a short waiting period between applying and when the license becomes valid. There may be blood test or premarital education rules in certain states.

## Fees and waiting period

A fee applies for the license. There is often a short waiting period (e.g., 24–72 hours) after application before the license is valid. The license is typically valid for a set period (e.g., 30–90 days); the marriage must be performed within that time and the signed license returned to the issuing office.
noise-complaint.md:
## Report noise

You can report ongoing or repeated noise issues online. Provide the address, type of noise, and when it occurs. For emergencies or immediate danger, call 911.

## Quiet hours

Local laws typically define quiet hours (e.g., 10 PM–7 AM). During these hours, excessive noise from music, construction, or other sources may be subject to enforcement.

## Frequently asked questions

:::dropdown{title="What counts as excessive noise?"}
Sustained loud music, construction outside allowed hours, and repeated loud parties may be considered violations.
:::

:::dropdown{title="How long until someone responds?"}
Non-emergency complaints are processed in order; response times vary by area and time of day.
:::
public-records-request.md:
## Submit a request

Submit a public records (Freedom of Information / open records) request to the agency that holds the records. Use the agency’s online form, email, or mail as specified on their website. Describe the records you want as clearly as possible so staff can locate them. You may receive a confirmation and a request number.

## What is available

Laws define which government records are public. Common examples include meeting minutes, budgets, contracts, permits, and certain correspondence. Some information is exempt (e.g., personal privacy, ongoing investigations). The agency will identify any withheld portions and the legal basis.

## Response time and fees

Agencies must respond within the time required by state or local law (often 5–30 business days). They may extend the time for complex requests and must notify you. Copy and search fees may apply; the agency will tell you the cost before fulfilling the request. Fee waivers or reductions are sometimes available for certain requesters or uses.
